conversations.content: Why is it that during harvest months some people's breathing is not bothered by the dust and pollen?

That is an interesting question, and something farmers often notice when working together in the fields. There are several reasons why, even in the same season and area, one person may feel perfectly fine breathing the air during harvest, while another may cough or struggle.

**1. Differences in immune sensitivity:** Some people's immune systems simply do not react strongly to pollen or fine agricultural dust. For them, the particles enter the nose or lungs, but their body does not treat them as a threat, so no inflammation occurs. Others have an overactive immune response, producing sneezing, runny nose, or shortness of breath.

**2. Gradual adaptation over time:** Farmers who have worked in the same fields, with the same crops and pollen, over many years sometimes develop what’s called ‘desensitization’. This means that repeated low exposure can train the immune system to stop reacting. In some cases, a person may have been sensitive in the past but, after many seasons, no longer feels those old symptoms.

**3. Local plant variety and pollen type:** Not all pollen is equally irritating. For example, the pollen from sorghum or certain vegetable flowers is heavier and less likely to stay airborne, while some grass and tree pollens travel farther and cause more reactions. If someone’s fields have more of the heavier-pollen plants—or if the wind patterns in their part of the valley blow the lighter pollen away—they may breathe cleaner air during harvest.

**4. Timing of fieldwork and environmental factors:** Early morning moisture, such as dew, causes pollen and dust to settle, making breathing easier. People who work mainly at these times may avoid the conditions that trigger others. Altitude, wind direction, and humidity also play a role—especially in mountainous areas like Ibb.

**5. Overall health and lifestyle:** Farmers who maintain strong lung health—through regular outdoor work, absence of respiratory illness, and good nutrition—often tolerate airborne particles better. Even something as simple as staying hydrated can help the airways function well and resist irritation.

In rural Yemen, it is not unusual for a person to find that their breathing during harvest becomes easier over the years. Sometimes it surprises them, especially if they remember a time when the air felt harsher. The body’s relationship with the land changes; just as our hands adapt to soil and tools, our lungs can adapt to the harvest air.

If you are curious, you might compare notes with neighbors—what crops they grow, when they harvest, and how they feel at different times of day. You might find there is a whole map of small differences in fields, schedules, and plant varieties that explain why some breathe more easily than others in the same season.

knowledge_query.preference: Past bout of malaria contracted during flooding season

conversations.content: Why do I notice more fever sickness spreading after the rivers overflow in our village?

That is a very good and important question, Sami. When heavy rain comes and the rivers overflow, the water often spreads into fields, paths, and low places where it sits for days or weeks. This standing water becomes the perfect place for certain mosquitoes – especially the Anopheles type – to lay their eggs.

These mosquitoes are the ones that can carry malaria, as well as other illnesses. They bite a person who is already infected and then spread the parasite when they bite someone else. After flooding, there are usually many more of these breeding spots than usual, so the mosquito population grows quickly. In addition, the flooding sometimes damages drainage channels and makes it harder for people to avoid being bitten, since the insects can enter homes more easily.

Besides mosquitoes, overflowing rivers can also spread bacteria and cause other water-borne diseases such as typhoid or cholera. This happens when floodwater mixes with waste or dirty sources, then people use it for washing, cooking, or even drinking without realizing it is contaminated.

For you and your neighbors, a few practical steps may help reduce the sickness: clearing puddles and small pools near your house and fields; using bed nets, especially at night; covering arms and legs in the evenings; and, if possible, repairing or improving small water channels so that water flows away instead of sitting still.

So the fevers you notice after the rivers spill over are not just coincidence – they are linked to changes in the environment that allow more disease-carrying insects and germs to thrive. Paying attention to these changes can help your family stay healthy during the rainy months.

conversations.content: In the colder months, I feel more tired and my knees ache more, even though my work in the fields is the same. Could it be something missing in what I eat then?

What you describe, brother Sami, can happen to many people when the season changes, especially in mountain areas like Ibb. It is not only the cold air making the joints stiff — sometimes it is connected to what the body is receiving from food and sunlight.

There is a kind of 'hidden helper' in our bodies that keeps bones strong and muscles working well. Our bodies can make it when sunlight touches the skin, and we also take some from certain foods like oily fish, eggs, or milk that has been enriched. In winter, the sun feels weaker, and we may spend more hours covered or inside, so our bodies make less of this helper. If our daily meals do not include enough of the foods that carry it, our stores can run low.

For someone like you who works outdoors but may wear full covering for warmth and modesty, sunlight may not reach much skin during these months. Also, many traditional village diets in Yemen have little access to fresh fish or fortified dairy in winter, so it is easy for this helper to become scarce. When levels are low, you can feel more tired, and bones or knees may ache — like how a plough works poorly if the oil inside has dried.

If possible, try to catch some gentle morning sun on your face and hands when it is warm enough, even for a short time. Adding an egg when you can, or some dried fish if it appears in the market, might help. Some people also take a supplement, but this is best discussed with a clinic worker — they can guide the right amount so it is safe and effective.

So yes, in the colder months your diet and reduced sunlight together might cause this missing piece — one that strengthens both bones and muscles. Taking small steps now could ease the aches and give you more steady energy through the season.
How would I know for sure if I’m missing this ‘hidden helper’?

The only certain way to know is through a simple blood test at a clinic — it measures how much of this helper is in your body. In Yemen, some clinics in towns can arrange this test, though not every rural post has it. Without the test, the signs you describe — tiredness, bone or joint ache worse in winter, and weaker muscles — can suggest low levels, but those signs can also come from other causes.

If it’s hard to get tested right away, a health worker can still assess you by asking questions and checking your bones and muscles during an exam. Based on that, they may suggest safe sunlight exposure and diet changes first, or a supplement for a limited period. This way you can start to improve even before you know the exact number.

It’s important not to take high-dose supplements on your own for many months, because too much of this helper can also cause harm. That’s why guidance from someone trained is best — they can help you know both whether you are missing it and how to restore it safely.

Many people think that simply spending time in the sun is enough for strong bones, because sunlight helps the body make a nutrient called vitamin D. This nutrient plays a role in helping your body use calcium from your food to keep bones healthy.

However, even if you work outside every day, the sun is only one half of the picture. To build and repair bones, your body also needs certain foods that either contain vitamin D directly or provide minerals that work with it, like calcium and phosphorus. If those foods are missing or not eaten often enough, you might still develop weakness in the bones over time.

Another thing to keep in mind is that sometimes clothing, time of day, or the season can limit how much sunlight your skin actually uses to make vitamin D. For example, in the early mornings or late afternoons, the sun’s rays are gentler, and your body may produce less vitamin D than at midday. Also, if your diet is mostly grains and vegetables, with little or no fish, eggs, or fortified dairy, you may not be getting enough to support what the sun gives you.

In Yemen’s rural areas, people sometimes face this when fish isn’t affordable or fresh milk is limited — especially in the dry season. Adding more foods like eggs from your own chickens, dairy if available, or even sun-dried small fish from the market when you can find them may help. Some beans and leafy greens can also support bone health when eaten with other nutrient-rich foods.

So yes, even a man who works under the sun may feel his bones grow weaker if certain foods are missing. Sunlight and diet need to work together, like water and soil for crops — each one by itself is not enough to make the harvest strong.

Third, other factors—like age, overall nutrition, or certain health conditions—can affect how your body uses the vitamin D it makes. Over time, low vitamin D can cause your muscles to feel more tired, your bones to ache, and small injuries to heal more slowly.

For someone in your situation, even simple steps can help: try to expose your forearms and face for 15–20 minutes around midday when possible, add a portion or two of foods rich in vitamin D if you can find them in the market (for example, dried fish or more eggs), and combine those with foods that have calcium, like yogurt. Little changes like these over several months often make a real difference. And of course, if the tiredness or pain worsens, it’s worth visiting a local clinic to check your levels.
